query: Japan 刑事訴訟法 再審 reform bill Diet schedule May 2026 衆議院 passage
answer:

# Japan 刑事訴訟法 (Criminal Procedure Code) Retrial (再審) Reform Bill — Status as of May 22, 2026

## Executive Summary

Japan's government-sponsored bill to amend the Code of Criminal Procedure (刑事訴訟法) to reform the retrial (再審) system was **approved by Cabinet on May 15, 2026** and **submitted to the House of Representatives (衆議院) the same day**. The bill is designated as bill number **衆第61号** of the 217th Diet session. According to NHK, **House of Representatives deliberation is scheduled to begin on May 26, 2026**. The bill has **not yet been voted on or passed** by the 衆議院 as of the latest available information (May 22, 2026). The current Diet session runs **through July 17, 2026**, and the government aims for passage during this session.

---

## 1. Legislative Timeline (Detailed Chronology)

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| **March 2024** | Cross-party Retrial Reform Diet Members' League (再審法改正議員連盟) formed, chaired by Masahiko Shibayama (柴山昌彦) |
| **September 2023** | Iwao Hakamada acquitted by Shizuoka District Court on retrial; acquittal confirmed October 2024 when prosecutors decline to appeal |
| **February 13, 2026** | Legislative Council (法制審議会) submits reform proposals to Justice Minister Hiroshi Hiraguchi (平口洋). Proposals include evidence disclosure but do **not** ban prosecutorial appeals. Vote: 12 for, 4 against, 1 abstention |
| **February 26, 2025** | Japan Federation of Bar Associations (日弁連) issues President's Statement demanding retrial reform in current Diet session |
| **Late March 2026** | LDP begins preliminary screening (事前審査) of government draft bill; members of cross-party league revolt against original draft |
| **April 6, 2026** | Government postpones planned Cabinet approval (originally targeted for ~April 7); LDP opposition forces delay |
| **April 9, 2026** | Government informs opposition parties that submission deadline cannot be met; pledges "best efforts" for current session |
| **April 22-23, 2026** | Justice Ministry signals willingness to ban prosecutorial appeals "in principle" after multiple revisions |
| **May 7, 2026** | Justice Ministry presents 2nd revised draft to LDP; places ban in supplementary provisions (付則). LDP lawmakers reject it, demanding main text (本則) placement |
| **May 9, 2026** | Mainichi reports Ministry begins considering upgrading ban to main provisions |
| **May 12, 2026** | Ministry finalizes 3rd revised draft; ban on appeals "in principle" placed in main text of bill |
| **May 13, 2026** | LDP Judicial Affairs Division & Research Commission on Judiciary System approve 3rd draft; Japan Innovation Party (coalition partner) also approves same day |
| **May 15, 2026** | **Cabinet approves** (閣議決定) the bill; submitted to House of Representatives the same day. Three opposition parties (Centrist Reform Alliance, Team Mirai, Communist Party) submit counter-bill calling for **total ban** on prosecutorial appeals |
| **May 20, 2026** | Activists rally on anniversary of 1975 Shiratori Decision |
| **May 26, 2026** | **Scheduled start of House of Representatives deliberation** (衆議院審議入り), per NHK |

---

## 2. Bill Details: Key Provisions

The bill (第217回衆第61号「刑事訴訟法の一部を改正する法律案」) contains the following core provisions:

### 2a. Prosecutorial Appeals — "Ban in Principle" (検察官抗告の原則禁止)
- **Deletes** the existing Code of Criminal Procedure clause allowing prosecutors to file immediate appeals (即時抗告) against retrial-granting decisions
- **Creates a new article** allowing prosecutorial appeals **only** when there are "sufficient grounds" (十分な根拠) to believe the retrial decision should be overturned
- Also tightens requirements for special appeals (特別抗告) to the Supreme Court: adds "sufficient grounds" requirement on top of existing constitutional violation/precedent conflict requirement
- Prosecutors must **promptly disclose** whether they have filed an appeal and the reasons for doing so
- Courts should try to conclude appeal proceedings **within one year**

### 2b. Evidence Disclosure Rules (証拠開示の制度化)
- Courts **must order** prosecutors to disclose "evidence relevant to the reasons for retrial requests" when deemed necessary
- Supplementary provision states judges should ensure scope of disclosure is "not unreasonably narrow"
- **Penalty** for misuse of disclosed evidence: up to **1 year imprisonment** or fine of up to **¥500,000** (~$3,100)

### 2c. Judge Recusal (裁判官の除斥・忌避)
- Judges who participated in the **original trial** or the investigations underlying it are **excluded** from retrial-request proceedings

### 2d. Procedural Improvements
- Courts can set hearing dates for retrial-request proceedings (either on application or ex officio)
- Screening mechanism introduced for early dismissal of cases lacking formal requirements
- **Five-year review** clause: the reformed law to be reviewed every five years after taking effect

---

## 3. Political Dynamics

### Government / LDP
- The bill is classified as a **"重要広範議案" (Important and Broad Bill)** — requiring the Prime Minister's attendance at questioning
- The unprecedented **three rounds of revision** during LDP preliminary screening was described by all major outlets as "異例" (exceptional)
- LDP Judicial System Research Commission chair **Keisuke Suzuki** (former Justice Minister) stated: "It became a good bill... now closer to what the public has been calling for"
- LDP lawmaker **Masahiko Shibayama** described the compromise as "gut-wrenching" but "half a step forward"
- LDP lawmaker **Takako Suzuki** (PR HQ chair) stated earlier: "A ban on appeals is the desperate cry of victims of wrongful convictions. Without a ban, there will be no revision"

### Opposition Parties
- On May 15, **Centrist Reform Alliance (中道改革連合)**, **Team Mirai (チームみらい)**, and **Japanese Communist Party (共産党)** jointly submitted a **counter-bill** to the House of Representatives calling for:
  - **Total ban** on prosecutorial appeals (no exceptions)
  - **Broader evidence disclosure** scope
  - **Removal** of the penalty for "off-purpose use" of disclosed evidence
- Opposition demands will be central to Diet deliberation; they are pushing for parallel deliberation of both bills and amendment negotiations

### Japan Federation of Bar Associations (日弁連)
- Has expressed concerns that:
  - Evidence disclosure scope ("relevant evidence") could be interpreted too narrowly
  - The penalty for off-purpose use of evidence could restrict media coverage and public right to know
  - The screening mechanism could dismiss meritorious cases too quickly
  - The "sufficient grounds" exception for prosecutorial appeals is a "loophole"

### Justice Ministry / Prosecutors
- Initially resisted any ban on prosecutorial appeals
- The Legislative Council (法制審議会) recommendation in February 2026 maintained the status quo on appeals
- Ministry was forced into unprecedented triple revision during LDP screening
- Critics note the Ministry secretariat is staffed mainly by prosecutors and has been accused of lobbying Diet members against reform

---

## 4. Current Status and Outlook (as of May 22, 2026)

- **Bill status**: Submitted to 衆議院 on May 15; **House deliberation scheduled to begin May 26**
- **Diet session deadline**: July 17, 2026
- **Key uncertainty**: Whether the government bill will be **amended during Diet proceedings** — opposition parties and legal advocates are pushing hard for modifications, especially on prosecutorial appeals and evidence disclosure
- The bill has **not yet passed the House of Representatives** and has approximately **7-8 weeks** remaining before the session ends
- After 衆議院 passage, the bill must also pass the **House of Councillors (参議院)**
- Nikkei noted "uncertainty regarding whether the bill will pass before the current Diet session concludes in two months"

---

## 5. Catalyst: The Hakamada Case

The reform was directly catalyzed by the case of **Iwao Hakamada** (袴田巖), age 90:
- Convicted of quadruple murder in 1966 in Shizuoka Prefecture
- Sentenced to death; spent **over 45 years on death row** — formerly the world's longest-serving death-row prisoner
- First retrial order in **2014**; took **9 additional years** due to prosecutorial appeals before retrial began
- **Acquitted** at retrial by Shizuoka District Court in **September 2023**; acquittal confirmed **October 2024**
- Key evidence: color photographs of "blood-stained clothing" were disclosed by prosecutors **29 years** after his first retrial petition — these proved inconsistencies in the prosecution's case

---

key_facts: Cabinet approved the Criminal Procedure Code retrial reform bill (刑事訴訟法の一部を改正する法律案) on May 15, 2026 and submitted it to the House of Representatives the same day
House of Representatives deliberation is scheduled to begin on May 26, 2026 (per NHK)
The bill has NOT yet passed the 衆議院 as of May 22, 2026
The current 217th Diet session runs through July 17, 2026
The bill bans prosecutorial appeals against retrial orders 'in principle' but allows exceptions when there are 'sufficient grounds'
The Justice Ministry was forced to revise the bill THREE times during LDP preliminary screening — described as unprecedented (異例)
Three opposition parties (Centrist Reform Alliance, Team Mirai, Communist Party) submitted a counter-bill on May 15 calling for a TOTAL ban on prosecutorial appeals
The bill is classified as a 'Important and Broad Bill' (重要広範議案) requiring PM Takaichi's attendance at questioning
The bill creates mandatory evidence disclosure rules for retrial proceedings for the first time
This would be the first revision to Japan's retrial system since the Code of Criminal Procedure was enacted in 1948/1949
The reform was catalyzed by the Hakamada case — Iwao Hakamada (age 90) spent 45+ years on death row before retrial acquittal in 2023/2024
The Legislative Council (法制審議会) submitted recommendations in February 2026 but did NOT recommend banning prosecutorial appeals — the ban was added due to LDP pressure
The cross-party Retrial Reform Diet Members' League was formed in March 2024 with over half of all Diet members joining
Violations of evidence disclosure rules carry penalties of up to 1 year imprisonment or ¥500,000 fine
Courts should aim to conclude any prosecutorial appeal within one year under the new rules
